Omega Centauri integrated in a Milky Way potential over 5Gyr using the IAS15 integrator.

Open Source

In some of my spare time, I’ve been revisiting a lot of the core material from undergraduate physics and astrophysics. Part of that process has been trying to bridge the gap between my current work in software engineering and the research areas I’d eventually like to move further into.

One of the ways I’ve approached that is through open source contributions in astrophysics software. I ended up choosing the galactic dynamics library galpy, partly because I used it during my undergraduate studies, and partly because the maintainers had already provided a list of interesting potential extensions and research-adjacent problems to work on.

It’s been a genuinely enjoyable way to learn again - reading papers, implementing methods, debugging numerical behaviour, and slowly becoming more comfortable working closer to the underlying physics rather than purely infrastructure and backend systems.

Hopefully this is just the beginning of a much longer list of projects.

Same orbit as above, in R-z plane.

IAS15 Numerical Integrator

One of my first major open source contributions involved implementing the IAS15 numerical integrator published in Rein & Spiegel 2015. IAS15 is a high-order adaptive integrator designed for extremely accurate long-term orbital integration, particularly in gravitational systems where numerical stability matters over very long timescales.

The project became a great introduction to the practical side of computational astrophysics — translating mathematical descriptions from papers into reliable code, understanding numerical error behaviour, and working within an established scientific codebase used by researchers.

One interesting challenge was adapting the integrator to align with the integration routines already present within the library. Unlike some other implementations, this version makes additional function calls during each timestep to calculate the current position, allowing compatibility with the library’s constant output timestep structure.

In practice, this means the adaptive integrator internally maintains accuracy over its own timesteps, while smaller (or larger) constant timesteps are exposed externally to the user. Although functional, this approach could likely be improved further through interpolation methods, reducing the need for repeated small-step evaluations while preserving accuracy guarantees.

More than anything, the project gave me a much deeper appreciation for how much careful engineering sits underneath the scientific software researchers often rely on every day without thinking twice about it.

Note: New methods for determining the timestep have been proposed in Pham et al. 2024 that would be valuable to implement in order to bring the method to feature parity.

Density profiles provide simplified models for understanding galactic structure and dark matter distributions.

Einasto Density Profile

Another project involved implementing the Einasto density profile (Einasto 1965) as a galactic potential. The Einasto profile appears frequently in studies of galactic structure and dark matter halo modelling, and has become an important tool in modern galactic dynamics.

The implementation itself was relatively straightforward, largely following the existing approaches within galpy for mass densities stratified on spherical geometries. I chose to follow the conventions described in Retana-Montenegro et al. (2012), which discusses several equivalent parameterisations of the Einasto density profile, including:

$$ \rho(r) = \mathrm{amp}\,\exp\left( -d_n \left[ \left(\frac{r}{r_s}\right)^\frac{1}{n} - 1 \right] \right) $$ $$ \rho(r) = \mathrm{amp}\,\exp\left( -2n \left[ \left(\frac{r}{r_{-2}}\right)^\frac{1}{n} - 1 \right] \right) $$ $$ \rho(r) = \mathrm{amp}\,\exp\left( -\left(\frac{r}{h}\right)^\frac{1}{n} \right) $$

Retana-Montenegro et al. provided a useful analytical expression for the potential as a function of radius which, combined with my own derivations of the radial force and second radial derivatives, allowed for the implementation of the common mechanics for a spherical density profile. My codes translated each of the intialisation conventions into a common backend, using the estimation equation (see paper) for $d_n$ if necessary and a numerical solver to refine this initial estimation. Many of the calculations performed above made use of known identities for the derivatives of the Gamma and incomplete Gamma functions, together with numerical libraries used to evaluate these functions efficiently.

One aspect of this project that I particularly enjoyed was the process of translating between the mathematics presented in the literature and the practical considerations of scientific software design. Even relatively compact equations often require a surprising degree of numerical care once they become part of a reusable library. Taking pen to paper throughout this project was also a really fun detour from the more software-oriented side of the work.

The conserved actions define a torus in euclidean space.

(WIP) Triaxial Stäckel Action Estimation

More recently, I’ve started exploring methods for approximating orbital actions, particularly the Triaxial Stäckel approximation method from Sanders & Binney 2015.

The method described in the paper leverages the analytical properties of action calculations in the presence of a Stäckel potential. The key then becomes finding a suitable Stäckel potential to describe the actual potential (locally, at least). It's this step that has piqued my interest, as I believe there may be some room for improvement on current methodology which uses integrated test particles to determine a suitable Stäckel potential - I'd love to be able to return to this work and find some new methods.

At the time of writing, my implementation of the original method is just a work in progress (you can view the open PR Here), but it’s become one of the most interesting areas I’ve looked at so far.

Over time I’d like this page to grow into a collection of projects, notes, implementations, and lessons learned while working closer to computational astrophysics.
